ad_hominem.name: Ad Hominem
ad_hominem.description: Attacking the person making the argument rather than the argument itself
improvements: Address the argument's claims directly instead of the person behind them
Replace character attacks with evidence-based counterpoints

strawman.name: Strawman
strawman.description: Misrepresenting someone's argument to make it easier to attack
improvements: Restate the opposing position accurately before critiquing it
Quote or paraphrase the original claim faithfully

appeal_to_nature.name: Appeal to Nature
appeal_to_nature.description: Arguing something is good or right because it is 'natural' (or bad because it is 'unnatural')
improvements: Provide empirical evidence for the claim rather than relying on what is 'natural'
Acknowledge that natural does not automatically mean beneficial

false_dilemma.name: False Dilemma
false_dilemma.description: Presenting only two options when more exist
improvements: Acknowledge additional alternatives or a spectrum of possibilities
Reframe the argument to consider middle-ground positions

slippery_slope.name: Slippery Slope
slippery_slope.description: Arguing that one thing will lead to a chain of negative events without evidence
improvements: Provide evidence for each step in the causal chain
Limit the claim to the immediate, demonstrable consequence

begging_the_question.name: Begging the Question (Circular Reasoning)
begging_the_question.description: Assuming the conclusion in the premises (a claim proves itself)
improvements: Support the conclusion with independent premises that don't restate it
Introduce external evidence or reasoning that stands on its own

appeal_to_authority.name: Appeal to Authority
appeal_to_authority.description: Using an authority figure as evidence without proper justification
improvements: Cite the actual data or study the authority relies on
Ensure the authority is a credible expert in the relevant field

appeal_to_ignorance.name: Appeal to Ignorance
appeal_to_ignorance.description: Claiming something is true (or false) because it has not been proven otherwise
improvements: Shift from absence of disproof to presenting positive evidence for the claim
Acknowledge what is currently unknown and propose how it could be tested

hasty_generalization.name: Hasty Generalization
hasty_generalization.description: Drawing a conclusion from insufficient evidence
improvements: Provide a larger or more representative sample before generalizing
Qualify the claim with words like 'some' or 'in certain cases'

post_hoc.name: Post Hoc Ergo Propter Hoc
improvements: Present a plausible causal mechanism, not just temporal sequence
Cite controlled studies or experiments that isolate the causal variable

appeal_to_emotion.name: Appeal to Emotion
appeal_to_emotion.description: Using emotional manipulation instead of logical reasoning
improvements: Replace emotional appeals with factual evidence and logical reasoning
Separate emotional framing from the core claim so each stands independently

bandwagon.name: Bandwagon (Appeal to Popularity)
bandwagon.description: Arguing something is true or good because many people believe it
improvements: Provide evidence beyond popularity to support the claim
Explain why the claim is correct on its own merits, regardless of how many believe it

false_cause.name: False Cause
false_cause.description: Incorrectly identifying the cause of an event or outcome
improvements: Identify and rule out alternative explanations for the effect
Reference studies with proper controls that isolate the proposed cause

red_herring.name: Red Herring
red_herring.description: Introducing irrelevant information to distract from the main argument
improvements: Remove tangential points and stay focused on the central claim
Clearly state the main thesis and ensure every supporting point relates to it

appeal_to_tradition.name: Appeal to Tradition
appeal_to_tradition.description: Arguing something is right because it has been done that way for a long time
improvements: Explain why the tradition is effective based on evidence, not longevity
Consider whether circumstances have changed since the tradition began

no_true_scotsman.name: No True Scotsman
no_true_scotsman.description: Changing the definition to exclude counterexamples to a universal claim
improvements: Define terms clearly upfront and stick to those definitions
Accept counterexamples and narrow the claim rather than redefining it

tu_quoque.name: Tu Quoque
tu_quoque.description: Dismissing criticism because the critic is allegedly guilty of the same thing
improvements: Evaluate the criticism on its own merits regardless of the critic
Address the substance of the objection with evidence

confirmation_bias.name: Confirmation Bias
confirmation_bias.description: Favoring information that confirms existing beliefs
improvements: Actively seek out evidence that contradicts the conclusion
Cite sources from opposing viewpoints and explain why the argument still holds

belief_perseverance.name: Belief Perseverance
belief_perseverance.description: Holding onto beliefs even after the evidence supporting them is discredited
improvements: Acknowledge when foundational evidence has been weakened or disproven
State what new evidence would change the conclusion

anchoring_bias.name: Anchoring Bias
anchoring_bias.description: Relying too heavily on the first piece of information encountered
improvements: Consider multiple reference points before drawing a conclusion
Test the claim against the full range of available data, not just the first data point

availability_heuristic.name: Availability Heuristic
availability_heuristic.description: Overestimating the probability of events with greater availability in memory
improvements: Supplement vivid examples with base-rate statistics
Ask whether the recalled examples are representative of the broader trend

hindsight_bias.name: Hindsight Bias
hindsight_bias.description: Seeing events as predictable after they have already happened
improvements: Describe what was actually known before the outcome occurred
Acknowledge uncertainty and the role of unpredictable factors

ingroup_bias.name: In-group Bias
ingroup_bias.description: Favoring members of one's own group over outsiders
improvements: Evaluate the claim using criteria that apply equally to all groups
Include perspectives and evidence from outside the in-group

dunning_kruger.name: Dunning-Kruger Effect
dunning_kruger.description: Overestimating one's own ability in a particular area
improvements: Reference domain experts and peer-reviewed sources rather than personal confidence
Acknowledge the limits of your own expertise on the topic

fundamental_attribution.name: Fundamental Attribution Error
improvements: Consider situational or systemic factors that could explain the behavior
Avoid attributing outcomes solely to character when context is relevant

sunk_cost.name: Sunk Cost Fallacy
sunk_cost.description: Continuing a behavior because of previously invested resources
improvements: Evaluate the claim based on future expected outcomes, not past investment
Ask whether you would still support the conclusion if starting fresh today

negativity_bias.name: Negativity Bias
negativity_bias.description: Giving more weight to negative experiences than positive ones
improvements: Present both positive and negative evidence in balanced proportion
Quantify outcomes rather than relying on emotional weight of negatives

self_serving_bias.name: Self-Serving Bias
self_serving_bias.description: Attributing successes to internal factors and failures to external factors
improvements: Apply the same causal standard to successes and failures alike
Consider external factors for successes and internal ones for failures as well

optimism_bias.name: Optimism Bias
optimism_bias.description: Overestimating the likelihood of positive outcomes and underestimating negatives
improvements: Include realistic risk assessment and potential downsides
Back up optimistic projections with historical base rates

survivorship_bias.name: Survivorship Bias
survivorship_bias.description: Concentrating on successful examples while overlooking failures, leading to false conclusions about what causes success
improvements: Include failed cases alongside successful ones for a complete picture
Ask what the full population looks like, not just the visible survivors

egocentric_bias.name: Egocentric Bias
egocentric_bias.description: Relying too heavily on one's own perspective or recalling events in a self-serving manner
improvements: Incorporate third-party perspectives or external data to verify the claim
Separate personal experience from objective evidence

empathy_gap.name: Empathy Gap
empathy_gap.description: Underestimating the influence of emotional or physical states on one's own or others' judgments and decisions
improvements: Acknowledge how emotional or physical states might color the judgment
Revisit the argument in a different emotional state to test its robustness

attribute_substitution.name: Attribute Substitution
attribute_substitution.description: Answering a difficult question by unconsciously substituting it with a simpler, related one
improvements: Make the actual question explicit and answer it directly with relevant evidence
Check that the reasoning actually addresses the original claim, not a simpler proxy

maslows_hammer.name: Maslow's Hammer
maslows_hammer.description: Over-relying on a familiar tool, framework, or theory and forcing it onto situations where it does not fit ('if all you have is a hammer, everything looks like a nail')
improvements: Consider whether the chosen framework genuinely fits the situation or is being forced
Try explaining the issue through at least one alternative lens before committing to a single theory

hypercognition.name: Hypercognition
hypercognition.description: Possessing too many categories or concepts for a domain, leading to over-classification and seeing distinctions or patterns that are not meaningfully present
improvements: Ask whether the fine-grained label adds explanatory value or just jargon
Simplify the classification and test whether the broader category explains the evidence just as well

russells_teapot.name: Russell's Teapot
russells_teapot.description: Making an unfalsifiable claim and shifting the burden of proof to others to disprove it, rather than providing evidence for the claim
improvements: Provide positive evidence for the claim rather than demanding others disprove it
Acknowledge the claim is speculative if no supporting evidence exists

symmetry_impulse.name: Symmetry Impulse
symmetry_impulse.description: Using symmetry or forced balance to complete judgments — assuming every positive must have an equal negative, every pro must have a con, or both sides of an issue deserve equal weight regardless of evidence asymmetry
improvements: Check whether the evidence actually supports equal weight on both sides before presenting a balanced view
Let the evidence determine the proportions rather than imposing artificial symmetry

proportionality_bias.name: Proportionality Bias (Major Event / Major Cause)
proportionality_bias.description: Assuming that the magnitude of a cause must match the magnitude of its effect — large events must have large causes, and small causes cannot produce large outcomes. This drives conspiracy thinking and dismissal of simple explanations for significant events
improvements: Consider that small causes can have large effects through cascading mechanisms (the butterfly effect)
Evaluate the causal chain on its own merits rather than matching cause size to effect size

all_or_nothing.name: All-or-Nothing Thinking
all_or_nothing.description: Viewing situations in black and white categories
improvements: Introduce degrees or a spectrum instead of absolute categories
Use qualifying language: 'tends to' or 'in most cases' rather than 'always/never'

overgeneralization.name: Overgeneralization
overgeneralization.description: Seeing a single negative event as a never-ending pattern
improvements: Limit the conclusion to the specific case rather than extrapolating universally
Provide additional data points before claiming a pattern

mental_filter.name: Mental Filter
mental_filter.description: Focusing exclusively on negative details while filtering out positive aspects
improvements: Include positive or neutral evidence alongside the negative
Present a balanced view by acknowledging what supports the opposing conclusion

discounting_the_positive.name: Discounting the Positive
discounting_the_positive.description: Rejecting positive experiences by insisting they 'don't count'
improvements: Explain why positive evidence is being discounted with objective criteria
Weight positive data the same way as negative data

catastrophizing.name: Catastrophizing
improvements: Assess the realistic probability of the worst-case scenario with data
Present a range of likely outcomes, not just the extreme negative

emotional_reasoning.name: Emotional Reasoning
emotional_reasoning.description: Believing that negative feelings reflect reality
improvements: Separate how you feel about the claim from what the evidence shows
Ground the argument in observable facts rather than emotional reactions

mind_reading.name: Mind Reading
mind_reading.description: Assuming you know what others are thinking without sufficient evidence
improvements: Replace assumptions about others' thoughts with their stated positions or actions
Cite direct quotes, surveys, or behavioral data instead of imputed motives

fortune_telling.name: Fortune Telling
fortune_telling.description: Predicting negative outcomes as if they are facts
improvements: Present predictions as possibilities, not certainties
Support forecasts with trend data or expert consensus

should_statements.name: Should Statements
should_statements.description: Using 'should' or 'must' statements that set unrealistic expectations
improvements: Replace prescriptive 'should' language with descriptive reasoning about trade-offs
Explain the rationale behind the expectation rather than asserting obligation

magnification_minimization.name: Magnification/Minimization
magnification_minimization.description: Blowing negative things out of proportion or shrinking positives
improvements: Assign proportional weight to evidence based on its actual significance
Use data to calibrate the magnitude of positives and negatives objectively

labeling.name: Labeling
labeling.description: Attaching negative labels to oneself or others
improvements: Describe specific behaviors or evidence instead of attaching labels
Avoid reductive characterizations and focus on what can be observed

personalization.name: Personalization
personalization.description: Assuming responsibility for events outside your control
improvements: Identify all contributing factors rather than assigning sole responsibility
Distinguish between correlation with a person and actual causal responsibility

occams_razor.name: Occam's Razor
occams_razor.description: The simplest explanation is usually the correct one
occams_razor.improvement: Simplify the argument by removing unnecessary assumptions and entities

hanlons_razor.name: Hanlon's Razor
hanlons_razor.description: Do not attribute to malice what can be adequately explained by mistake or ignorance
hanlons_razor.improvement: Consider non-malicious explanations before attributing intent

hitchens_razor.name: Hitchens's Razor
hitchens_razor.description: What can be asserted without evidence can be dismissed without evidence
hitchens_razor.improvement: Back every key assertion with at least one piece of concrete evidence

sagan_standard.name: Sagan Standard
sagan_standard.improvement: Match the strength of your evidence to the scale of your claim

newtons_flaming_laser_sword.name: Newton's Flaming Laser Sword
newtons_flaming_laser_sword.description: If something cannot be settled by experiment or observation, it is not worth debating
newtons_flaming_laser_sword.improvement: Propose a concrete way to test or observe the claim

poppers_falsifiability.name: Popper's Falsifiability
poppers_falsifiability.description: A claim should be testable and potentially falsifiable to be meaningful as an empirical claim
poppers_falsifiability.improvement: State explicitly what evidence would disprove the claim
